items.title: Mechanism of action of aminoglycosides
items.contentHtml:
Mechanism of action of aminoglycosides

Aminoglycosides (gentamicin, amikacin) bind to the 30S ribosomal subunit, specifically to the 16S rRNA site, causing misreading of the mRNA and the incorporation of incorrect amino acids. This generates abnormal proteins that insert into the cell membrane, altering its permeability and causing bacterial death. Its action is concentration-dependent bactericidal (peak/MIC >8-10). They require oxygen-dependent active transport, so they are ineffective in anaerobiosis. The prolonged post-antibiotic effect allows for single daily doses.

items.foco: Ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity due to aminoglycosides
items.title: Ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity
items.contentHtml:
Ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity due to aminoglycosides

Toxicity is due to accumulation in cells of the renal proximal tubule and in the hair cells of the inner ear. Nephrotoxicity manifests as acute non-oliguric tubular necrosis, with an increase in creatinine after 5-7 days. Ototoxicity can be cochlear (irreversible hearing loss at high frequencies) or vestibular (vertigo, nystagmus). Risk factors: duration >7 days, high doses, renal failure, concomitant use of furosemide or vancomycin. Monitor plasma levels (peak and trough) and serial audiometry.

items.title: Post-antibiotic effect
items.contentHtml:
Post-antibiotic effect of aminoglycosides

The post-antibiotic effect (PAE) is the persistent suppression of bacterial growth after exposure to the drug, even when serum concentrations are below the MIC. In aminoglycosides it lasts 2-8 hours and is directly proportional to the peak concentration. It justifies once-daily dosing, maximizing bactericidal efficacy and reducing toxicity by allowing drug-free periods. EPA is longer against aerobic gram-negative bacilli than against Staphylococcus.

items.foco: Macrolide resistance due to ribosome methylation
items.title: Macrolide resistance due to methylation
items.contentHtml:
Macrolide resistance due to ribosome methylation

Mechanism of acquired resistance due to methylation of residue A2058 in the 23S rRNA of the 50S subunit, mediated by methyltransferases enzymes encoded by erm genes (erythromycin ribosome methylase). This conformational modification prevents the binding of macrolides, lincosamides and streptogramins B, generating the MLSB resistance phenotype (constitutive or inducible). It is the main cause of therapeutic failure in Streptococcus pyogenes and Staphylococcus aureus. Detectable by D test (adjacent erythromycin and clindamycin disc).

items.foco: Use of macrolides in atypical infections
items.title: Macrolides in atypical infections
items.contentHtml:
Use of macrolides in atypical infections

Macrolides (azithromycin, clarithromycin, erythromycin) are first-line drugs for infections caused by atypical intracellular pathogens: Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Chlamydia pneumoniae, Chlamydia trachomatis, Legionella pneumophila and Bordetella pertussis. They act by binding to the 50S subunit, inhibiting peptide translocation. In community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) they are used in combination with beta-lactams or as monotherapy in outpatients without comorbidities. Azithromycin has greater intracellular penetration and long half-life (68 h).

items.foco: Prolonged QTc syndrome due to macrolides
items.title: QTc prolonged by macrolides
items.contentHtml:
Prolonged QTc syndrome due to macrolides

Macrolides (especially erythromycin and azithromycin) block the hERG potassium channel (IKr), prolonging ventricular repolarization and the QTc interval. The risk of torsade de pointes is higher if baseline QTc >450 ms in men or >470 ms in women, or if combined with other drugs that prolong QT (antipsychotics, class III antiarrhythmics, fluoroquinolones). Risk factors: hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, bradycardia, liver failure. Contraindicated in congenital long QT syndrome. Monitor ECG if risk factors.

items.title: Mechanism of action of tetracyclines
items.contentHtml:
Mechanism of action of tetracyclines

Tetracyclines (doxycycline, minocycline, tigecycline) bind reversibly to the 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking the binding of aminoacyl-tRNA to the A site of the ribosome. This inhibits the elongation of the peptide chain. They are bacteriostatic and broad spectrum, including atypical bacteria, spirochetes and some anaerobes. Tigecycline (glycylcycline) evades resistance mechanisms through efflux pumps and ribosomal protection. They require energy-dependent active transport and chelate divalent cations (Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+), interfering with their oral absorption.

items.foco: Phototoxicity and bone deposits due to tetracyclines
items.title: Phototoxicity and bone deposits
items.contentHtml:
Phototoxicity and bone deposits due to tetracyclines

Phototoxicity is an exaggerated skin reaction to UV light, with erythema, edema and blisters in exposed areas, more common with doxycycline (high doses). The mechanism involves absorption of UV radiation by the drug, generating free radicals. Tetracyclines are deposited in bones and developing teeth forming complexes with calcium phosphate, causing enamel hypoplasia and permanent yellow-brown discoloration. Therefore they are contraindicated in pregnancy and lactation. minocycline can cause skin and thyroid hyperpigmentation.

items.title: Contraindication in children
items.contentHtml:
Contraindication of tetracyclines in children

Tetracyclines are contraindicated in children under 8 years of age due to their affinity for calcium in calcifying tissues. They cause permanent tooth discoloration (yellow-gray-brown) and enamel hypoplasia. They can also inhibit bone growth by forming insoluble complexes in the epiphyseal plate. Doxycycline is used exceptionally in children <8 years old for rickettsiosis and Lyme disease (short duration), since its chelation is lower. In people over 8 years of age the dental risk is low. Safe alternatives: macrolides or beta-lactams.

items.foco: Mechanism of action of chloramphenicol and aplastic anemia
items.title: Chloramphenicol and aplastic anemia
items.contentHtml:
Mechanism of action of chloramphenicol and aplastic anemia

Chloramphenicol binds to the 50S ribosomal subunit, inhibiting peptidyltransferase, blocking the formation of the peptide bond. It is bacteriostatic (bactericidal against H. influenzae and N. meningitidis). Aplastic anemia is an idiosyncratic, rare but fatal reaction (1/25,000-40,000), with irreversible medullary panhypoplasia, mediated by damage to the mitochondrial DNA of stem cells. It is not dose-dependent. It also produces reversible dose-dependent medullary toxicity (erythroid vacuolization). Its use is restricted to serious infections with no alternative (typhoid fever, H. influenzae meningitis).

items.title: Linezolid and MAOIs
items.contentHtml:
Interaction of linezolid with MAOI

Linezolid (oxazolidinone) is a reversible inhibitor of monoamine oxidase (MAOI) (type A and B), although with low potency. When combined with classical MAOIs (phenelzine, tranylcypromine) or with serotonergic drugs (SSRIs, SNRIs, triptans, tramadol, dextromethorphan), it can precipitate a serotonin syndrome (hyperthermia, rigidity, myoclonus, confusion). Caution: avoid concomitant use and respect a 14-day washout after discontinuing linezolid if an MAOI is started. In emergencies, monitor for signs of toxicity. It is bacteriostatic against enterococci and staphylococci (including methicillin-resistant S. aureus).

items.foco: Bacteriostatic vs bactericidal effect of protein inhibitors
items.title: Bacteriostatic vs bactericidal
items.contentHtml:
Bacteriostatic vs bactericidal effect of protein inhibitors

Most protein synthesis inhibitors are bacteriostatic (tetracyclines, macrolides, chloramphenicol, linezolid, tigecycline), that is, they inhibit bacterial growth without cell lysis. Exceptions: aminoglycosides are concentration-dependent bactericides (they alter membrane). Chloramphenicol is bactericidal against H. influenzaeand N. meningitidis. Clinically, in severe infections (endocarditis, meningitis, febrile neutropenia) bactericides are preferred. Bacteriostatics can be effective if the immune system is intact. The combination of bacteriostatic + bactericidal can be antagonistic (e.g. chloramphenicol + beta-lactam).
